description:
A small CSV-to-JSON data wrangling step is needed before you copy today’s sensor snapshot to the IoT gateways.

    1. You will find an input CSV file at  
       /home/user/device_data/devices.csv  
       (this file already exists).

    2. Filter that CSV so that **only the rows whose “temp_c” column value is strictly higher than 70** are kept.  
       • “temp_c” is a decimal number (the file can be treated as plain text; no header quoting issues).  
       • Retain each qualifying row’s “id” and “location” fields only; ignore the rest of the columns.

    3. Emit the filtered result as a JSON array and write it to  
       /home/user/output/overheat_devices.json  

       Required JSON formatting rules that the automatic checker will enforce exactly:  
       • The file must contain a single JSON array.  
       • The array must be on one physical line (no pretty-printing / no embedded newlines).  
       • Each element is an object with exactly two string properties, ordered as follows:  
         id, then location.  
         Example element: {"id":"dev-003","location":"lab-south"}  
       • There must be no trailing spaces anywhere in the file.

    4. Write an execution log (plain text) to  
       /home/user/output/overheat_devices.log  

       The log must consist of exactly two lines, each terminated with a single newline character “\n”:  
       • Line 1: the total number of records read from the CSV (an integer).  
       • Line 2: the number of records that appear in overheat_devices.json (an integer).

    Hints: standard Unix text utilities or a short Python/Node/Ruby one-liner are all acceptable, but remember the final JSON must follow the single-line format above.

    When you are done, the system should contain the two new files mentioned, and their contents must strictly follow the given rules so that the automated tests can match them byte-for-byte.
truth:
Initial filesystem state that the grading environment prepares:

    1. File: /home/user/device_data/devices.csv  
       Contents (including the header line):

       id,location,temp_c,battery_v  
       dev-001,roof-nw,68.3,3.7  
       dev-002,roof-se,72.4,3.8  
       dev-003,lab-south,75.0,3.6  
       dev-004,lab-north,70.0,3.9  
       dev-005,warehouse,71.2,3.8  

    2. Directory /home/user/output/ exists and is writable by the user.

    Expected artefacts AFTER successful task completion:

    1. File: /home/user/output/overheat_devices.json  
       Exact byte-for-byte content:  
       [{"id":"dev-002","location":"roof-se"},{"id":"dev-003","location":"lab-south"},{"id":"dev-005","location":"warehouse"}]

       (one single line, no trailing newline)

    2. File: /home/user/output/overheat_devices.log  
       Exact byte-for-byte content, including the final newline at the end of line 2:  
       5  
       3  

    File permissions are not graded, only existence and exact content.
